summary: Joel Spolsky outlines seven (plus one bonus) principles for delivering customer service so good that customers remark about it. Drawing from Fog Creek's experience as a bootstrapped company, he argues that fixing problems at their root (not just symptom-level), taking blame gracefully, offering generous refund policies, and hiring highly qualified support staff creates fanatically devoted customers. He uses vivid anecdotes—a locksmith, a diner owner, Lands' End—to illustrate how emotional dynamics in customer interactions matter as much as the technical fix. The core thesis is that investing more upfront in quality support is cheaper long-term than handling the same problems repeatedly with cheap outsourced labor.
key_points: Fix every support problem twice: once for the customer and once in the product, treating each incident like an NTSB crash investigation to eliminate entire classes of problems permanently.
Generous refund policies (90-day no-questions-asked) cost Fog Creek only 2% of revenue while eliminating chargebacks entirely and removing customer anxiety that leads to hostility.
Taking blame with phrases like 'it's my fault' instantly defuses angry customers—avoiding lawsuits comes from not having angry people, not from never admitting fault.
Customer service staff must be highly qualified and co-located with developers, with a real career path, because once common problems are eliminated only hard debugging remains.
Treat angry customer interactions like puppetry: the customer is yelling at the business, not at you personally, so focus on what response will make them happy.
money_quotes: We treat each tech support call like the NTSB treats airliner crashes.
When customers have a problem and you fix it, they're actually going to be even more satisfied than if they never had a problem in the first place.
The cheap call centers have no mechanism for getting problems fixed; indeed, they have no incentive to get problems fixed because their income depends on repeat business.
We were able to increase our sales tenfold while only doubling the cost of providing tech support.
The way to avoid getting sued is not to have people who are mad at you.
You will never win an argument with your customer.
Our customers are nice because they're not worried. They're not worried because we have a ridiculously liberal return policy.
Say 'It's my fault' a hundred times one morning in the shower, until it starts to sound like syllabic nonsense. Then you'll be able to say it on demand.
key_insight: Investing in high-quality, root-cause-fixing customer service costs more per incident but dramatically less overall, while turning customers into vocal advocates.
